english: (C.: *kụn*) all, every, each; whole; spui kún-bu kún-nas (spu'i kun bu kun nas) from every pore Dzl.; dé-dag kun (de dag kun) all these; ɣżan kun (gzhan kun) all the others; also pleon. kun tʻams-ċàd (kun thams cad) all of them, they altogether; kún-gyis mtʻóṅ-ba, tʻós-pa (kun gyis mthong ba, thos pa) seen, heard by every body, generally known; kún-tu (kun tu) 1. into all, in all etc. 2. adv. everywhere, in every direction; kun-tu-bzaṅ-po (kun tu bzang po) Allgood, n. of the first of the celestial Bodhisattwas, Samanta-bhadra, sometimes confounded with Adibuddha, tʻóg-mai Saṅs-rgyas (thog ma'i sangs rgyas); in later works even a Kun-tu-bzaṅ-mo (kun tu bzang mo) Yum (yum) is mentioned Thgr.; kún-tu rgyú-ba (kun tu rgyu ba) to go everywhere, wander about; Kun-tu-rgyu (kun tu rgyu) परिव्राजक n. of a class of Brahmans, itinerant monks, Dzl.; kún-nas (kun nas) from everywhere, round about, wholly, thoroughly e. g. overpowered by passions, cleansed from sin Dzl.; kún-nas ʽdod-pa (kun nas 'dod pa) to wish from the bottom of the heart Thgy. Comp. kun-dkrís (kun dkris) general corruptness, misery, sin Lex. -- Kun-kʻyáb (kun khyab) comprising, pervading all things. -- Kun-mkʻyén-(pa) (kun mkhyen (pa)) omniscient. -- Kun-dgá-bo (kun dga' bo), Ssk. ānandā, n. of the favourite disciple of Buddha; Kun-dga (kun dga') is to this time frequently used as a name of (female) persons. -- Kun-dga-rá-ba (kun dga' ra ba), also kun-dga (kun dga') Thgy., or kun-ra (kun ra), Ssk. आराम or संघाराम 'garden of all joys' 1. the grove in which a monastery is situated. 2. the monastery. 3. in Tibet, which is destitute of groves, more particularly the auditory or library of a monastery -- Kun-brtágs (kun brtags), in the Mahayana: a personal, erroneous supposition Was. -- Kun-ʽdús (kun 'dus) all-gathering, all-uniting. -- Kun-dbaṅ (kun dbang) almighty. -- Kun-rdzób (kun rdzob) altogether vain, delusive; kún-rdzób-kyi bdén-pa (kun rdzob kyi bden pa) subjective truth Was. -- Kun-ɣżi (kun gzhi) lit.: the primary cause of all things, viz.: 1. the soul or spirit, kun-ɣżii sems (kun gzhi'i sems) (opp. to ʽbyuṅ-ba bżii lus ('byung ba bzhi'i lus) the body consisting of the 4 elements), kun-ɣżii séms-la pʻo mo ma mčís-te (kun gzhi'i sems la pho mo ma mchis te) as no difference of sex exists in souls (we, though being women, would beg etc.) Mil. 2. With more precise distinction: kun-ɣżi (kun gzhi) soul as the seat of the passions, opp. to sems-nyid (sems nyid), the very soul, the spirit as the seat of reason Mil. 3. To the followers of the Adibuddha doctrine kun-ɣżi (kun gzhi) is = God, Adibuddha, kun-ɣżii Saṅs-rgyas (kun gzhi'i sangs rgyas). -- Kun-rzigs (kun gzigs) all-seeing. -- Kun-slóṅ (kun slong) Lex. v. sloṅ-ba (slong ba).
also kum-kúm (kum kum), kúm-po (kum po), crooked, shriveled, dried up; *kum tún-ċe* (*kum tun ce*) W. to bend together, to double. (Cf. skúm-pa (skum pa)).
also kerketana & ketaka Ssk. 'n. of a precious white stone' Cs.; our Ssk. dictionaries give but the last of these names, and as its only signification the name of the tree Pandanus odoratissima.
Sambh., n. of a mountain, prob. Kedāra, part of the Himalaya. Will.
Ssk. a fiery meteor, shooting star; the descending node.

wylie: ke la sha, kai la sha
english: Cs., कैलास Will., n. of a lofty region of the Himalaya, mythological rather than geographical, seems to be the same as Ti-se (ti se) q. v., though modern geographers apply the name to different ranges.

the fraternity or association, which Huc mentions under this name (Voy. II ch. 6), seemed to be totally unknown to our Tashilhunpo Lama, although the expectation of a final war between Buddhist believers
